region_of_origin: China (Teochew / Chaoshan region); widely adopted across Singapore and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Plain white rice cooked in excess water (1:10 rice-to-water ratio) for 30–45 minutes until grains partially dissolve into a smooth, thin porridge

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of plain Teochew-style rice congee weighs approximately 350g and is the typical single-serving portion consumed at a Singaporean Chinese breakfast or as a light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Rice Porridge (Congee) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for plain rice porridge / congee. Values represent the cooked, plain (no toppings) product at the standard 1:10 rice-to-water ratio typical of Teochew-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Congee / White Rice Porridge
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~78 (high) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice congee / porridge. Prolonged boiling of white rice at high water ratios increases starch gelatinisation and raises GI relative to steamed rice (~64). GL per 350g bowl = (78 × 21g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.4 (medium GL category). Available carbohydrate per bowl: 6.0g/100g × 350g = 21g.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content when prepared plain (2mg/100g; ~7mg per 350g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Plain Teochew congee is a very low-sodium food and poses no hypertension risk on its own; however, clinicians should counsel patients that common toppings and condiments — salted eggs, preserved vegetables, salted fish, and soy sauce — can collectively add 800–1,500mg sodium per meal, which is the primary HTN concern in congee-based meals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~78) but medium glycemic load (~16.4) per standard 350g bowl due to high water dilution of starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain congee has a high GI (~78) but a medium glycemic load (~16.4) per standard 350g bowl because the high water content limits available carbohydrate to ~21g per serving; T2DM patients may consume one standard bowl of plain congee as part of a balanced meal, but should avoid multiple servings, high-GI toppings such as you tiao, or high-sugar condiments that substantially increase the overall glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Virtually zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and negligible sodium in the plain preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Teochew congee is a heart-friendly base food with negligible saturated fat (0g/100g), zero cholesterol, and minimal sodium; CVD patients should be counselled that the cardiovascular risk in congee meals arises entirely from toppings such as salted fish, lard-fried you tiao, and preserved meats, not from the plain congee itself.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No pro-inflammatory nutrients; easily digestible and gentle on the GI tract
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain congee contains no fat, no sodium, and no known pro-inflammatory nutrients, making it an appropriate gentle food during RA flares or post-corticosteroid gastrointestinal upset; its high water content also supports hydration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain rice congee (白粥, bái zhōu) — also called Teochew porridge, muay, or jook — is one of the most fundamental foods in Asian cuisine. It is prepared by simmering white rice in approximately 10 times its weight in water for 30–45 minutes until the grains partially dissolve into a smooth, thin, translucent porridge with a mild, neutral flavour and a silky, watery texture. The Teochew-style version (稀饭, xī fàn) is characteristically thinner and less starchy than Cantonese congee (jook), which is thick and milky. Both styles share similar nutrient profiles but differ in texture and water content. Nutritionally, plain congee is extremely low in energy (28 kcal/100g; ~98 kcal per 350g bowl), very low in protein (0.5g/100g), and very low in fat (0.1g/100g), with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient (6g/100g; ~21g per bowl). It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and dairy-free. Clinically, plain congee is frequently mischaracterised as 'bad for diabetics' because of its high GI (~78). The correct clinical counselling is that plain congee has a HIGH GI but a MEDIUM glycemic load (~16.4) per standard 350g bowl because the high water content limits available carbohydrate to approximately 21g per serving — comparable to 1–2 slices of wholemeal bread. The real glycemic and cardiovascular risk in congee meals arises from toppings: century egg (sodium), preserved radish (sodium), salted fish (sodium + saturated fat), and you tiao (deep-fried, high GI). Plain congee is an appropriate hospital meal for post-operative T2DM patients, elderly patients with chewing difficulties, and acutely unwell patients requiring easily digestible food. In Singapore, Teochew-style congee is a traditional Chinese breakfast served with small side dishes of pickles, preserved vegetables, and protein toppings.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are all defensible exact matches. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean Chinese dish are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for plain rice congee are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation; these sub-fractions are therefore unavailable from any approved source and recorded as -1 per numeric hygiene rules. water_g set to -1: moisture content is not explicitly reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for plain rice congee; estimated water content is very high (~90g/100g) given the 1:10 rice-to-water cooking ratio, but this value is not confirmed by a named approved source and is therefore recorded as -1 per numeric hygiene rules. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. saturated_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source — plain congee contains no added fats and the trace fat (0.1g/100g) in white rice is predominantly unsaturated. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food containing no animal products. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are confirmed 0.0 per HPB source — plain white rice congee contains negligible fat-soluble and water-soluble vitamins. Two duplicate portions in the input (both 350g, both is_default=true) have been consolidated to a single canonical serving of 350g. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database portal and is an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.1), sodium_mg (2.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for plain rice congee at 1:10 water ratio and consistent with HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (78), gi_category (high), glycemic_load_per_serving (16.4), gi_source fully populated. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database — approved GI source. GL calculation documented: (78 × 21g) / 100 = 16.4.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03310748 (rice porridge) — exact match. FoodEx2 A00HU (Congee / rice porridge) — exact match. SNOMED CT 226529007 (Rice porridge substance) — exact match. All three anchors are defensible and internally consistent.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,650 characters). Covers food identity, preparation method, texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (GI/GL counselling for T2DM, HTN, CVD), and key nutrients. Suitable for RAG and LLM training.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required.
